data.title: KONE Oyj reports Q1 results
data.description:
• KONE Oyj press release (KNYJF): Q1 GAAP EPS of €0.41.
  • Revenue of €2.71B (+1.5% Y/Y).

data.title: Iberdrola reports Q1 results
data.description:
• Iberdrola press release (IBDSF): Q1 net profit increased to €1.865B up ~11% Y/Y
  • Revenue of €12.02B (-0.2% Y/Y).

data.title: Oruka Therapeutics prices upsized $700M public offering at $72.50 per share
AstraZeneca beats top-line and bottom-line estimates; reaffirms FY26 outlook
data.description:
• AstraZeneca PLC press release (AZN): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $2.58 beats by $1.31.
  • Revenue of $15.29B (+12.5% Y/Y) beats by $370M.
  • Core operating profit increased 12%.
  • Core tax rate of 21%. Expectations for full year Core Tax rate are unchanged at 18-22%.
  • Guidance: AstraZeneca reconfirms total revenue and core EPS guidance for FY 2026 at CER, based on the average foreign exchange rates through 2025. Total revenue is expected to increase by a mid-to-high single-digit percentage vs. estimated growth of 7.56% Y/Y. Core EPS is expected to increase by a low double-digit percentage vs. estimated growth of 12.04% Y/Y. The core tax rate is expected to be between 18% and 22%.

data.title: PureTech reports FY results, will delist ADS from Nasdaq
data.description:
• PureTech press release (PRTC): FY  Consolidated cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments of $277.3M as of December 31, 2025.
  • As of March 31, 2026, PureTech level cash and cash equivalents were $248.1M.
  • The company also announced that it has notified Nasdaq of its intention to voluntarily delist its ADSs, each representing the right to receive 10 ordinary shares of the company.
  • After delisting, PureTech’s (PRTC) ordinary shares will remain listed on the London Stock Exchange, which will continue to serve as the company's primary trading venue.

data.title: Geely Q1 profit drops on FX impact; NEV international sales surge 100% Y/Y
data.description:
Geely's (GELYF) total revenue in the first quarter reached 83.78B yuan, an increase of 15% from the same period last year. Total vehicle deliveries reached a record 709,358 units in the quarter.

Net profit fell to about 4.17B yuan in the three months ended March 31, primarily due to adverse foreign-exchange movements.

The company’s Hong Kong filing  showed that while reported earnings declined, core profit—a metric excluding certain one-offs—rose 31% to 4.56 billion yuan, beating expectations and signaling underlying operational strength.

New-energy vehicle (NEV) momentum remained strong, with sales climbing to 369,059 units, accounting for more than half of total deliveries.

NEV sales achieved a 9% Y/Y growth. The performance in overseas markets was remarkable, with overseas sales in Q1 touching 203,024 units, surging more than 100% Y/Y and serving as a core driver.

data.title: Caesars targets sub-5x lease-adjusted leverage as it reiterates path to $500M+ digital EBITDA
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Caesars Entertainment (CZR) Q1 2026

Management view

  • Caesars opened the call with Q1 scale and segment highlights, with President & COO Anthony Carano saying, “consolidated net revenues of $2.9 billion increased $77 million or 3% year-over-year,” and “Adjusted EBITDAR of $887 million improved by $3 million over the prior year.”

  • On Las Vegas trends, Carano said, “we experienced a significant sequential improvement in the hospitality vertical in Q1 with occupancy of 95.3% in the quarter,” while adding, “leisure trends were still down on a year-over-year basis.” He tied the near-term setup to group demand, saying, “for the remainder of 2026, we continue to forecast sequential improvement in Las Vegas operating trends driven by strong group and convention mix and stabilizing leisure trends.”

  • On regionals, Carano highlighted the Canada transaction and the end of the large regional CapEx cycle, saying, “On March 3, we closed on the acquisition of Caesars Windsor,” and “with the completion of our Tahoe master plan scheduled in June 2026, we will have successfully completed all major large planned regional CapEx projects since the completion of the merger back in 2020.”

  • On digital, President Caesars Sports and Online Eric Hession said, “Caesars Digital delivered record first quarter net revenue and adjusted EBITDA of $374 million and $69 million, respectively,” and added, “we continue to see a business capable of achieving 20% top line revenue growth with 50% flow-through to EBITDA.”

  • CFO Bret Yunker emphasized the Windsor purchase and free cash flow setup: “on March 3, we acquired the operations of Caesars Windsor for USD 54 million and entered into a 20-year operating agreement with the Ontario Lottery and Gaming Corporation,” and “We expect to deliver strong free cash flow in 2026 during the balance of the year as a result of continued operating momentum, lower cash interest expense and lower CapEx.”

  • CEO Thomas Reeg framed the year as a pivot toward cash generation and capital return, saying, “With the completion of our capital cycle, we're in a free cash flow harvesting stage now,” and “you should expect as we move forward… that we'd be back to a balance between debt paydown and stock repurchase.”

Outlook

  • Reeg updated near-term cadence for Las Vegas, saying, “April was a little softer than we anticipated, largely because we didn't hold like we did last year,” and “I'd say we'll still likely be just short of last year.”

  • He reiterated second-half comparisons as a potential tailwind, saying, “then we cycle into comps versus last summer as everybody remembers that was a tough summer in Vegas.”

  • For Digital, Reeg pointed to cost relief from expiring deals: “we have some significant partnership expenses that roll off in '26,” adding, “The bulk of those benefits will flow to us in the third and fourth quarter of this year and then into the first quarter of '27.”

Financial results

  • Carano reported Las Vegas segment performance as, “adjusted EBITDAR of $426 million versus $433 million last year on flat revenues,” alongside “occupancy of 95.3% in the quarter” and “ADR growth of 1%.”

  • Carano said Regionals delivered “net revenues of $1.4 billion” and “adjusted EBITDAR of $435 million,” and Reeg added, “last year, we had the Super Bowl in New Orleans… a little over $10 million of incremental EBITDA that obviously didn't repeat.”

  • Hession detailed Digital operating metrics, including, “Flow-through during the quarter was strong at just over 66%,” “EBITDA margins expanded 566 basis points to 18.4%,” and “our total monthly unique players increased approximately 2% to 512,000.”

Q&A

  • Daniel Politzer, JPMorgan: asked to “parse out the market” in Las Vegas leisure vs. group; CEO Reeg: “leisure market has continued to get healthier… each quarter since third quarter of last year,” while adding, “we still do have weeks that are soft.”

  • Daniel Politzer, JPMorgan: asked how Caesars weighs buybacks vs. debt; CEO Reeg: “the answer has typically been some mix of share repurchase and debt repayment.”

  • Brandt Montour, Barclays: asked about regional consumer pressure; CEO Reeg: “the regional consumer has been remarkably resilient.” Montour also asked about getting back to “20%” digital growth; President Hession: “if you have mid-single-digit handle growth and then the iCasino side continuing to grow like it is, that's how we can get to that 20% range.”

  • Elizabeth Dove, Goldman Sachs: asked about “bringing value back to Vegas”; President & COO Carano: “we've taken a look at price up and down all of our properties.” CEO Reeg: “We were over 95% occupancy this quarter.”

  • Barry Jonas, Truist: asked if all-inclusive packages are breakeven; CEO Reeg: “We're not pricing anything to breakeven.” Jonas asked about VICI lease coverage; Reeg: “when the 2 of us have something to report, I'll come back to you.”

  • Jordan Bender, Citizens JMP: asked about labor contracts; CEO Reeg: “Nothing to talk about at this point. New Jersey comes up this summer. Vegas is not till '28.”

  • Raymond Bowers, Wells Fargo: asked about leverage threshold for buybacks; CEO Reeg: “we want our leverage to be sub-5x on a lease-adjusted basis.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative-to-neutral, pressing on Las Vegas demand durability and pricing (“parse out the market,” “bringing value back”), capital return priorities, and lease coverage (VICI).

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks (“Happy with the start to the year, a strong quarter for us”), while answers were more guarded on specific sensitivities and unresolved items (“I'd rather not get that granular,” “I'm not going to keep updating every quarter”).

  • Versus last quarter, Reeg’s Las Vegas framing shifted from “coming off a very, very soft summer” (Q4) to “Vegas is obviously in a much healthier spot” (Q1), while maintaining the same pattern of strength on “big events and groups” and softness in “weeks that are soft.”

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Las Vegas messaging tightened from Q4’s emphasis on “very, very soft summer” leisure pressure to Q1’s emphasis on sequential improvement and stabilization, with Reeg saying, “the downdraft… are in the rearview mirror.”

  • Digital remained anchored to the same long-term model (“20% top line… 50% flow-through”), but Q1 added more emphasis on the competitive advantage of Caesars Rewards vs. peers facing higher acquisition costs, with Reeg saying, “the bulk of our customer acquisition comes from our Caesars Rewards database.”

  • Capital allocation language was consistent (mix of debt paydown and repurchase), but Q1 was more explicit about intra-year pacing, with Reeg saying, “you'll see in the first quarter, we didn't buy back stock.”

Risks and concerns

  • Reeg highlighted volatility in Las Vegas weekly demand, saying it remains “a tale of a very, very strong market when big events and groups are in town and softness when that isn't the case,” and he tied Q2 risk to hold: “April was a little softer… because we didn't hold like we did last year.”

  • On regionals, Reeg pointed to lapping the Super Bowl benefit in New Orleans, saying it was “a little over $10 million of incremental EBITDA” last year that “didn't repeat.”

  • On the VICI lease topic, Reeg indicated limited interim disclosure, saying, “when the 2 of us have something to report, I'll come back to you.”

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 as a steadier operating setup driven by Las Vegas group strength and record Digital profitability, while acknowledging week-to-week volatility in Vegas and a Q2 hold-related headwind. Leadership emphasized a transition into a “free cash flow harvesting stage,” with capital allocation returning to a debt paydown and repurchase mix, and reiterated longer-term Digital ambitions (“$500 million or more of EBITDA”) alongside a stated leverage objective of “sub-5x on a lease-adjusted basis.”

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: GSK beats top-line and bottom-line estimates; reaffirms FY26 and 2031 outlook
data.description:
• GSK press release (GSK): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of £0.465  beats by £0.03.
  • Revenue of £7.63B (+1.5% Y/Y) beats by £20M.
  • Specialty Medicines sales £3.2 billion (+14%); Respiratory, Immunology & Inflammation £0.9 billion (+16%); Oncology £0.5 billion (+28%); HIV sales £1.8 billion (+10%).
  • Vaccines sales £2.1 billion (+4%); Shingrix £1.0 billion (+20%); Meningitis vaccines £0.3 billion (-3%); and Arexvy £0.1 billion (-18%).
  • General Medicines sales £2.3 billion (-6%); Trelegy £0.6 billion (stable).
  • Cash generated from operations of £1.4 billion with free cash flow of £0.8 billion.
  • Q1 2026 dividend of 17p declared; 70p expected for full year 2026.
  • £1.7 billion executed to date as part of the £2 billion share buyback programme announced at FY 2024.
  • 2026 guidance and 2031 sales outlook reaffirmed: Expect 2026 turnover growth of between 3% to 5%; Core operating profit growth of between 7% to 9%; Core EPS growth of between 7% to 9%.
  • 2031 sales outlook of more than £40 billion.

data.title: Landstar signals April truck revenue per load about 13% above April 2025 while providing Q2 commentary instead of formal guidance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Landstar System, Inc. (LSTR) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Against that backdrop, the Landstar business model performed well with revenue increasing approximately 2% compared to the 2025 first quarter, gross profit increasing approximately 14%, variable contribution dollars increasing approximately 7% and basic and diluted earnings per share increasing approximately 36%.” (President, CEO & Director Frank Lonegro)
  • “We are driving to incorporate AI into our business and do everything we can to mitigate any perceived industry-specific AI disintermediation risk.” (President, CEO & Director Lonegro) “Initial feedback points to meaningful time savings, higher shipment life cycle throughput and improved visibility across the network.” (President, CEO & Director Lonegro)
  • “One consistent highlight in our results remains the strength of our industry-leading unsided platform equipment business.” (President, CEO & Director Lonegro) “We generated approximately $134 million of heavy-haul revenue during the 2026 first quarter, representing an 18% increase over the 2025 first quarter.” (President, CEO & Director Lonegro)
  • “During the 2026 first quarter, the company returned approximately $104 million to shareholders through our capital return programs.” (President, CEO & Director Lonegro) “The company returned approximately $82 million in dividends to stockholders during the first quarter and deployed approximately $22 million to share repurchases during the first quarter.” (President, CEO & Director Lonegro)
  • “The 2026 first quarter gross profit was $112.5 million compared to gross profit of $98.3 million in the 2025 first quarter.” (VP, Principal Accounting Officer & CFO James Todd) “Insurance and claims costs were $35.6 million in the 2026 first quarter compared to $39.9 million in 2025.” (VP, Principal Accounting Officer & CFO Todd)

Outlook

  • “Given the highly fluid freight transportation backdrop and a volatile geopolitical and macroeconomic environment, the company will be providing second quarter financial and operational commentary rather than formal guidance.” (President, CEO & Director Lonegro)
  • “The number of loads hauled via truck in April 2026 was essentially equal to April 2025 on a dispatch basis, while revenue per load in April was approximately 13% above April 2025 on a profit basis.” (President, CEO & Director Lonegro)
  • “Historically, the company has often experienced a 25 to 45 basis point compression in variable contribution margin from the first quarter to the second quarter, primarily driven by mix.” (President, CEO & Director Lonegro)

Financial Results

  • “Overall truck revenue per load increased 5.6% in the 2026 first quarter compared to 2025 first quarter.” (VP, Principal Accounting Officer & CFO Todd) “Non-truck transportation service revenue in the 2026 first quarter was 19% or $16 million below the 2025 first quarter.” (VP, Principal Accounting Officer & CFO Todd)
  • “Transportation and Logistics segment revenue was up 2% year-over-year on a 4% increase in revenue per load, partially offset by a 3% decrease in volume compared to the 2025 first quarter.” (VP, Principal Accounting Officer & CFO Todd)
  • “Operating income increased as a percentage of both gross profit and variable contribution as we cycle the impact of the international supply chain fraud matter in the 2025 first quarter, lower insurance and claim costs in the 2026 first quarter and the impact of the company's fixed cost infrastructure.” (VP, Principal Accounting Officer & CFO Todd)
  • “We ended the quarter with cash and short-term investments of $411 million.” (VP, Principal Accounting Officer & CFO Todd) “Cash flow from operations for the 2026 first quarter was $78 million and cash capital expenditures were $6 million.” (VP, Principal Accounting Officer & CFO Todd)

Q&A

  • Jonathan Chappell, Evercore ISI: “your 1Q volumes were down 2% year-over-year, 2% sequentially… is that market as strong as it's been portrayed?” President, CEO & Director Lonegro: “if we see an incremental uptick in demand, you're going to see it on the volume side.”
  • Scott Group, Wolfe Research: “is it realistic to think that we see a meaningful further acceleration from that 13% in April… and… how should we think about margin?” VP, Principal Accounting Officer & CFO Todd: “we are seeing above seasonal pricing strength here into April… both on the BCO side and the brokerage side.”
  • Scott Group, Wolfe Research: “BCO volume up 7% and brokerage volume down 9%… what do you think is driving… and… Supreme Court case…?” VP, Principal Accounting Officer & CFO Todd: “the agents are going to sell what they can sell.” President, CEO & Director Lonegro: “we're watching it like everybody else and we'll be prepared whichever way it goes.”
  • Christian Wetherbee, Wells Fargo: “what do we need to see from a spot perspective” for BCO adds? President, CEO & Director Lonegro: “When BCOs see multiple months in a row of sustained rate improvement, the word gets around pretty quick.”
  • Paul Stoddard, Goldman Sachs: “With your unique structure… how does that set you up versus peers” on the Supreme Court case? President, CEO & Director Lonegro: “the entirety of the brokerage population is going to have to look at insurance very differently if the decision goes against the industry than they do today.”
  • J. Bruce Chan, Stifel: data center exposure? VP & Chief Corporate Sales, Strategy & Specialized Freight Officer James Applegate: “It represents about 12% of our total revenue.”
  • Stephanie Benjamin Moore, Jefferies: “any foreign dispatch” exposure? President, CEO & Director Lonegro: “All of our agents are U.S. domiciled agents. We do have a handful of agents who do some back office work overseas.”
  • Brian Ossenbeck, JPMorgan: AI specifics and expense impact? President, CEO & Director Lonegro: “we have 7 active pilots.” VP & Chief Corporate Sales, Strategy & Specialized Freight Officer Applegate: “We started off with 6. We got about 10 stages that we're going to get within the shipment life cycle.”
  • Harrison Bauer, Susquehanna: carrier vetting and cargo theft/insurance link? President, CEO & Director Lonegro: “we have… put a higher degree of rigor around vetting our carriers.” VP and Chief Safety & Operations Officer Matthew Miller: “We're up to, say, dozens of attributes that we're looking at to scrub that carrier database.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone, centered on whether volume can follow pricing and how mix and legal/regulatory outcomes could affect margins, reflected in questions like “is it realistic… further acceleration” and the repeated focus on the Supreme Court case. (Scott Group, Wolfe Research)
  • Slightly positive management tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing improving momentum and confidence, including “The tone and positivity… was the best I've experienced during my tenure at Landstar.” (President, CEO & Director Lonegro)
  • Versus Q4, management language shifted from caution on demand (“the challenging demand conditions… continued”) to stronger inflection framing, including “I feel pretty convinced we're at the beginning of the beginning.” (President, CEO & Director Lonegro)

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q1 continued the shift away from formal guidance, with Q1 stating “the company will be providing second quarter financial and operational commentary rather than formal guidance,” compared with Q4’s “providing first quarter revenue commentary rather than formal guidance.” (President, CEO & Director Lonegro)
  • Q1 highlighted improvement in insurance and claims versus Q4’s elevated charges; in Q1, “Insurance and claims costs were $35.6 million,” while Q4 had “Insurance and claim costs were $56.1 million.” (VP, Principal Accounting Officer & CFO Todd)
  • AI discussion moved from strategy framing in Q4 (“approximately 50% of our IT CapEx budget for 2026 is dedicated to AI”) to Q1 deployment detail (“we have 7 active pilots”). (President, CEO & Director Lonegro)

Risks and Concerns

  • “We are monitoring the news on the geopolitical conflict in the Middle East and the related volatility in energy and diesel prices.” (President, CEO & Director Lonegro)
  • “Tariffs have certainly already impacted freight flows.” (President, CEO & Director Lonegro)
  • “This is something that today is a constant defense” against fraud and theft risk, with mitigation via tighter vetting and technology. (VP and Chief Safety & Operations Officer Miller)

Final Takeaway

Landstar management emphasized improving pricing and network sentiment, citing April truck revenue per load “approximately 13% above April 2025” alongside continued strength in heavy haul and ongoing AI pilots intended to increase shipment life-cycle throughput. The company maintained its approach of Q2 commentary rather than formal guidance, flagged typical seasonal mix-driven margin compression risk, and highlighted tighter carrier vetting and anti-fraud investments as key mitigants amid ongoing cargo theft and broader macro uncertainty.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Sensata expects Q2 revenue of $950M-$980M while maintaining a 19% annual margin floor
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sensata Technologies Holding plc (ST) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered revenue and adjusted operating income at the high end of our guidance range, and we exceeded our expectations on adjusted EPS and free cash flow." (CEO & Director Stephan Von Schuckmann)

  • "Free cash flow of $105 million was again a bright spot, as this represented 83% conversion" and "Return on Invested Capital... now stands at 10.8%." (CEO & Director Von Schuckmann)

  • "Despite multiple headwinds, including precious metals inflation of over 100%, our first-quarter Adjusted Operating Margin improved by 30 basis points year-over-year to 18.6%." (CEO & Director Von Schuckmann)

  • "Our outgrowth accelerated to 4% in the first quarter" and "we are prepared to act swiftly to preserve our margins in the event that automotive end-markets deteriorate." (CEO & Director Von Schuckmann)

  • "We have increased conviction around our right to win in data centers" and "adoption of liquid cooling is expected to accelerate beginning around mid-2027." (CEO & Director Von Schuckmann)

  • "We reported first-quarter revenue of $935 million" and "Adjusted earnings per share was $0.86." (CFO & Executive VP Andrew Lynch)

Outlook

  • "For the second quarter, we expect revenue of $950 million to $980 million" and "Adjusted Earnings Per share of $0.89 to $0.95." (CFO & Executive VP Lynch)

  • "Due to geopolitical uncertainty and end-market volatility, we are continuing our practice of providing guidance one quarter at a time." (CFO & Executive VP Lynch)

  • "We do want to share our view that current consensus estimates for Adjusted Operating Margin expansion of approximately 30 basis points per quarter in the back half are consistent with our view, provided that end-market demand holds up." (CFO & Executive VP Lynch)

  • "Should end-market demand deteriorate materially, we are prepared to take reasonable measures to defend the 19% annual margin floor that we committed to last year." (CFO & Executive VP Lynch)

  • Compared with the prior call’s broader directional framing ("We currently expect low single digits year-over-year revenue growth" and "we reiterate our annual margin floor of 19%"), management reiterated one-quarter-at-a-time guidance and tied the back-half margin cadence more explicitly to demand stability. (CFO & Executive VP Lynch, Q4 2025)

Financial Results

  • "We reported first-quarter revenue of $935 million" and "Adjusted Operating Income was $174 million and Adjusted Operating Margin was 18.6%." (CFO & Executive VP Lynch)

  • "Adjusted earnings per share was $0.86" and "We delivered $105 million of free cash flow in the quarter." (CFO & Executive VP Lynch)

  • "This was the final quarter of meaningful revenue impacts from the initiatives we began in 2024 to exit $200 million of annual revenues related to underperforming products." (CFO & Executive VP Lynch)

  • Segment performance and mix highlights: "All 3 of our segments delivered organic revenue growth and operating margin expansion" including Automotive "operating margin was 23.5%," Industrials "operating margin was 27.1%," and Aerospace, Defense, and Commercial Equipment "Operating Margin was 28.1%." (CFO & Executive VP Lynch)

  • Capital allocation and leverage: "We returned $43 million of capital to shareholders" and "Our net leverage ratio at the end of the first quarter was 2.65x trailing 12 months Adjusted EBITDA." (CFO & Executive VP Lynch)

Q&A

  • Ryan Choi (on for Wamsi Mohan), Bank of America: Asked for regional color on auto outgrowth and whether it’s sustainable; CEO & Director Von Schuckmann: "they are winning meaningful business in each and every region" and "we've got a strong conviction that the team will outgrow the market in 2026."

  • Ryan Choi (on for Wamsi Mohan), Bank of America: Asked about the margin step-up; CFO & Executive VP Lynch: "If your question is relating to Q2... the head start on the year and stronger productivity earlier in the year gives us a stronger lift."

  • Mark Delaney, Goldman Sachs: Pressed on navigating volatility and expanding margins; CEO & Director Von Schuckmann: "we have a clear playbook to respond" and "Sensata is prepared"; CFO & Executive VP Lynch: "the playbook around offsetting those is exactly the same."

  • Mark Delaney, Goldman Sachs: Asked for 2027 data-center revenue and margins; CEO & Director Von Schuckmann: "we'll share more progress"; CFO & Executive VP Lynch: "we're not at a point where we're providing a dollar revenue forecast."

  • Christopher Glynn, Oppenheimer: Asked if data center is catch-up vs limited current-gen opportunity; CEO & Director Von Schuckmann: "the opportunity is not as strong... in comparison to liquid-cooled data-center concepts" and "will start showing revenue growth... around mid-2027."

  • Joseph Giordano, TD Cowen: Probed magnitude of China outgrowth opportunity; CFO & Executive VP Lynch: China price-down is "mid-single-digits" and content growth needs to be "high-single-digit" to outgrow.

  • Guy Hardwick, Barclays: Asked about HVAC outperformance and sustainability; CFO & Executive VP Lynch: "That was primarily driven by the new content... our A2L product" and "we built an operating plan that was... does not rely on market growth."

  • Irvin Liu (for Amit Daryanani), Evercore ISI: Asked CapEx trajectory; CFO & Executive VP Lynch: "We're still targeting CapEx in the 3% to 3.5% range"; CEO & Director Von Schuckmann described sourcing equipment in "Southeast Asia or even China" to "optimize our CapEx."

  • Irvin Liu (for Amit Daryanani), Evercore ISI: Asked data-center TAM; CEO & Director Von Schuckmann: "Look, I think that question we'll take with us for the next call."

  • Joseph Spak, UBS: Asked on tariffs/IEEPA refunds and magnitude; CFO & Executive VP Lynch: "we're not seeing any meaningful direct impact" from metal tariff changes, expects "approximately $8 million per quarter" tariff run-rate, and on refunds "we're not going to speculate"; also "We paid a little over $40 million in tariffs last year."

  • Shreyas Patil, Wolfe Research: Asked why Q2 growth guide isn’t higher; CFO & Executive VP Lynch: Aerospace growth "likely moderates" and Industrials recovery is "until the back half of the year."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, repeatedly stress-testing durability of auto outgrowth, margin cadence under volatility, tariff uncertainty, and the lack of quantified data-center revenue/TAM (e.g., "can you give us a sense on what the total TAM... could look like"). (Evercore ISI)

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive and resilience-focused in prepared remarks ("we are prepared") and stayed measured in Q&A by emphasizing playbooks and scenario planning while declining to quantify certain upside ("we're not at a point where we're providing a dollar revenue forecast"). (CEO & Director Von Schuckmann; CFO & Executive VP Lynch)

  • Versus the prior quarter’s confidence around the reorganization and multi-year growth vectors, the current call carried more explicit caution tied to geopolitics, oil-price sensitivity, and tariff-rule uncertainty, while keeping the same margin-floor commitment. (CEO & Director Von Schuckmann; CFO & Executive VP Lynch)

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Transformation messaging shifted from building the new 3-segment structure and growth frameworks to execution proof points: Q1 emphasized "organic growth in each of our segments" and working-capital-driven cash conversion, versus Q4’s emphasis on reorganizing and outlining growth vectors such as data centers and UAVs. (CEO & Director Von Schuckmann)

  • Guidance posture remained quarter-by-quarter; Q1 added specificity on tariff run-rate changes ("approximately $8 million per quarter") and excluded potential IEEPA refunds from guidance, whereas Q4 framed tariffs and precious metals as headwinds with mitigation toolkits without similar run-rate detail. (CFO & Executive VP Lynch)

Risks and Concerns

  • Demand and geopolitics: "end-market demand risks that are posed by geopolitical events and the effects on oil prices." (CEO & Director Von Schuckmann)

  • Input costs: "precious metals inflation of over 100%" with mitigation via hedging ("about 80% hedge coverage" through the first half), supplier negotiations, redesign/VAVE, and customer discussions on compensation. (CEO & Director Von Schuckmann; CFO & Executive VP Lynch)

  • Tariff policy uncertainty: Q2 guidance is based on policies "in effect as of April 27, 2026" and "does not include any potential tariff refunds." (CFO & Executive VP Lynch)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a continuation of its transformation execution, highlighting $935 million revenue, $0.86 adjusted EPS, and $105 million free cash flow alongside segment-level organic growth and margin expansion. For Q2, management guided to $950 million-$980 million revenue and $0.89-$0.95 adjusted EPS, reiterated scenario-based actions to defend profitability, and maintained readiness to protect its 19% annual margin floor amid geopolitical, demand, and tariff-policy uncertainty. Data centers were positioned as a medium-term growth vector with spec-in progress and an expected liquid-cooling adoption acceleration beginning around mid-2027, while management declined to provide a quantified revenue/TAM forecast yet emphasized limited incremental investment needs to pursue the opportunity.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
